title: The Limo

jokes.text: "You hungry? Tuna sandwich is $9. You don't like it, go back to your own country."
jokes.setup: Airport stores having their own pricing system
jokes.punchline: Extreme xenophobic response to sandwich pricing complaints
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational comedy with escalating absurdity. The xenophobic punchline is unexpected and perfectly captures airport pricing frustration.

jokes.text: I think the whole airport-airline complex is a huge scam just to sell the tuna sandwiches
jokes.punchline: Entire aviation industry exists to support sandwich sales
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld conspiracy theory absurdism. Takes the setup to an extreme logical conclusion.

jokes.text: The terminals, the airplanes... it's all just a distraction... so that you don't notice the beating that you're taking on the tuna
jokes.setup: Previous conspiracy theory about airline industry
jokes.punchline: Elaborate misdirection scheme for tuna sandwich profits
jokes.explanation: Continues the established bit with good escalation, though less punchy than the initial premise.

jokes.text: Clock interaction sequence - man refuses to use his own watch despite wearing one
jokes.punchline: Illogical insistence on using distant clock instead of own watch
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment showing everyday social dysfunction, though not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: "We're living in a society!"
jokes.punchline: Indignant appeal to social norms while acting antisocially
jokes.explanation: Iconic Seinfeld line. Perfect economy, memorable delivery, became cultural catchphrase. The irony is perfectly constructed.

jokes.text: "One mile from the exit, it starts shaking, violently shaking... like it's having a nervous breakdown"
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing car as having mental health crisis
jokes.explanation: Perfectly George-ian way of describing car trouble. Character-specific neurotic observation.

jokes.text: "So you have no car." "No." "So, what good are you?"
jokes.punchline: Jerry's blunt assessment of George's reduced utility
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry-George dynamic. Brutal honesty delivered matter-of-factly. Great economy and character integration.

jokes.text: "If he knew O'Brien, he wouldn't have a sign"
jokes.setup: George worries chauffeur might know O'Brien
jokes.explanation: Smart logical reasoning that advances the plot while being funny. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: "What's gonna happen? They can't kill us."
jokes.punchline: Casual dismissal that will prove ironically wrong
jokes.explanation: Gains significance on rewatch given the Nazi plot twist. In the moment, it's standard Jerry confidence.

jokes.text: Irish name negotiation - George wants to be Dylan Murphy instead of just himself
jokes.punchline: George's desire for a cooler Irish identity
jokes.explanation: Pure George behavior - can't just be himself even in a simple con. Character-driven absurdity.

jokes.text: "Hey, do you have the time?" "Clock over there."
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that works through repetition, though not particularly inventive.

jokes.text: George calls his mother from limo - entire conversation about not being able to explain
jokes.setup: George wants to brag about being in limo
jokes.punchline: Escalating frustration as he can't explain the situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect George-mother dynamic. His need to impress versus inability to explain creates sustained cringe comedy.

jokes.text: "She happy for you?"
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and delivery. Jerry's deadpan response to George's obvious failure is classic their dynamic.

jokes.text: Testing if driver can hear by suggesting picking up his sister for fun
jokes.setup: Need to test if partition is soundproof
jokes.explanation: Functional joke but relies on mild transgression rather than clever construction.

jokes.text: "My dear fellow, where are you dropping us?" in affected accent
jokes.explanation: George's pretentiousness manifesting as bad accent work. Character-appropriate but not exceptional.

jokes.text: George attempts to quote George Bernard Shaw and completely mangles it
jokes.setup: George trying to sound intellectual after success
jokes.punchline: Completely butchers famous Shaw quote about seeing things as they are
jokes.explanation: Perfect George moment - trying to be profound and failing spectacularly. The confused repetition is brilliant character work.

jokes.text: "That's right, baby doll."
jokes.setup: Jerry feeling cool in the limo
jokes.explanation: Jerry trying on persona that doesn't fit him. Good character moment showing how the situation affects him.

jokes.text: "What other members of our party? I didn't even know we were in a party."
jokes.explanation: George's tendency to miss context and interpret things literally creates natural wordplay.

jokes.text: "It was a bad jig. We never should've started it." "It was a good jig." "It was a bad jig. A terrible, terrible jig."
jokes.punchline: Escalating insistence on jig quality assessment
jokes.explanation: The word 'jig' becomes unexpectedly funny through repetition and George's emphatic delivery.

jokes.text: "First, you don't look like any O'Brien." "You should've been O'Brien."
jokes.setup: George panicking about not looking Irish
jokes.punchline: Blame-shifting to Jerry for casting decisions
jokes.explanation: George's tendency to blame others when his own ideas go wrong. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: "Who are you, Mannix?" in response to jump and roll suggestion
jokes.setup: Jerry suggests jumping from moving car
jokes.punchline: Reference to 1960s detective show known for action sequences
jokes.explanation: Perfect pop culture reference that captures the absurdity of Jerry's action-hero suggestion.

jokes.text: "Oh, yes, he's very proud of his work in The Big Game."
jokes.setup: Fans mention O'Brien's book 'The Big Game'
jokes.punchline: Jerry's awkward attempt to play along without knowledge
jokes.explanation: Standard impersonation comedy - Jerry trying to fake knowledge he doesn't have.

jokes.text: Elaine's salary interrogation and Jerry's response about knowing how much George makes
jokes.punchline: Jerry knowing George's salary without George's permission
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamics showing how well Jerry knows George, perhaps too well.

jokes.text: Kramer demonstrates 360 dunk, presumably failing spectacularly in confined space
jokes.explanation: Classic Kramer physical comedy. Visual gag that plays to character's impulsive nature.

jokes.text: "You see a limo go by, you know it's either a rich guy... or 50 prom kids with $1.75 each."
jokes.explanation: Perfect Seinfeld observational comedy. Specific detail about split costs makes it memorable and relatable.

jokes.text: "Most of the time, you feel like... you're in some depressing single guy's apartment from, like, 1975."
jokes.explanation: Vivid imagery that deflates limo mystique. The specific year adds authenticity.

jokes.text: "You're counting, there must've been 10,000 asses already on this seat."
jokes.explanation: Continues the deflation of limo glamour with specific gross-out detail.

jokes.text: "Think someone's been tipped off?" in response to missing tip-off
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that gains significance with the Nazi plot revelation. Clever in context.

jokes.text: Elaine's fake praise for 'The Big Game' - "it's changed my life... left me breathless... brilliant man"
jokes.setup: Asked about book she's never read
jokes.explanation: Elaine's attempt to fake sophistication. Becomes more cringe knowing what the book actually contains.

jokes.text: "The fate of the world depends on the outcome of this game."
jokes.setup: Quote from what they think is sports book
jokes.punchline: Actually Nazi propaganda about racial conflict
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. What sounds like sports hyperbole is actually sinister political rhetoric. Dark comedy masterpiece.

jokes.text: "O'Brien? Why would he wanna be called O'Brien?"
jokes.setup: Kramer confused by Jerry's fake name
jokes.punchline: Innocent question that starts revealing the truth
jokes.explanation: Kramer's innocent confusion begins the revelation sequence. Good plot advancement through character.

jokes.text: "And the Jews steal our money... through their Zionist-occupied government..."
jokes.setup: George reading what he thinks is a speech
jokes.explanation: Shocking revelation that recontextualizes everything. Brave comedy writing that uses Nazi material for legitimate satirical purpose.

jokes.text: "You're not gonna open with that, are you?"
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response to horror. Jerry's practical concern about speech structure while ignoring content is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: "I'm ready to die for you."
jokes.explanation: Escalates the Nazi devotion while showing George's uncomfortable situation.

jokes.text: "Nice-looking Luger."
jokes.punchline: George's polite comment about Nazi gun
jokes.explanation: George's tendency toward inappropriate politeness in extreme situations. Perfect character moment in dark context.

jokes.text: Kramer's conspiracy theory about Jerry being too clean and organized
jokes.setup: Trying to figure out Jerry's connection to O'Brien
jokes.punchline: Jerry's cleanliness as evidence of Nazi tendencies
jokes.explanation: Kramer's paranoid logic linking Jerry's personality traits to fascism. Absurd but follows weird internal logic.

jokes.text: "Jerry's a Nazi?" "I can't even believe I didn't see it."
jokes.punchline: Kramer's certainty about Jerry's secret identity
jokes.explanation: Kramer's absolute conviction about his ridiculous theory. Character-driven absurdity that pays off.

jokes.text: "Kind of a cute Nazi, though."
jokes.setup: Jerry points out Eva is a Nazi
jokes.explanation: Perfectly awful George moment. His shallow priorities override moral concerns. Dark comedy gold.

jokes.text: George's hotel bed story - hurt hamstring trying to kick out tight sheets
jokes.setup: Can't run due to bad hamstring
jokes.explanation: Perfect George story - mundane situation escalated by his neuroses into injury. Relatable hotel complaint.

jokes.text: "It's for a mental patient." about tight hotel sheets
jokes.explanation: George's tendency to find sinister explanations for minor inconveniences. Good escalation.

jokes.text: "The Jews hate grass. They always have, always will."
jokes.punchline: Bizarre antisemitic conspiracy about artificial turf
jokes.explanation: Absurdly specific antisemitic theory about lawn care. Shows how bigotry can blame anything on targeted groups.

jokes.text: "Okay?" after George establishes hierarchy with Tim
jokes.setup: George asserting authority as supposed Nazi leader
jokes.explanation: George's insecurity showing even when pretending to be authoritarian leader. Character consistency.

jokes.text: "No one's ever been shot in the city."
jokes.setup: They can't shoot us in the city
jokes.explanation: Perfect George logic - creating comforting false narratives. Ironically delivered as obvious lie.

jokes.text: "Always so clean and organized." as evidence Jerry is a Nazi
jokes.explanation: Continues Kramer's paranoid logic. Funny because it's both wrong and weirdly plausible given his reasoning.

jokes.text: "He's just neat." - Elaine defending Jerry
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response to Kramer's absurdity. Economy and logic versus conspiracy.

jokes.text: "And you don't look like a Murphy." "I may not look like one... but I act like a Murphy."
jokes.punchline: Jerry's defense based on behavioral traits
jokes.explanation: Jerry's attempt to defend his fake identity through performance rather than appearance.

jokes.text: "He's extremely Murphy. To a fault."
jokes.explanation: George's tendency to oversell when lying. 'To a fault' is perfect overstatement.

jokes.text: Jerry's fake Irish backstory ending with 'Cereal famine. Couldn't get a bowl anywhere.'
jokes.punchline: Confusing potato famine with breakfast cereal shortage
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misremembering of Irish Potato Famine as cereal shortage. Perfect Jerry-esque confusion of serious history with trivial concerns.

jokes.text: "The peat." delivered with fake Irish reverence
jokes.explanation: Continues the fake Irish persona with appropriately pretentious detail.

jokes.text: "There's no real person can act the way he does." about George
jokes.punchline: Kramer questioning if George's personality could be real
jokes.explanation: Meta joke about George's character being so extreme it seems fake. Self-aware comedy about sitcom character construction.

jokes.text: "George and Jerry, they probably know who killed Kennedy."
jokes.setup: Conspiracy theory about Jerry and George
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Kramer's paranoid logic to ultimate conspiracy theory.

jokes.text: "How's tricks, Murphy?" from cross-eyed man
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy setup that creates confusion about who's being addressed.

jokes.text: "He was talking to me. He's cross-eyed. It could be very confusing."
jokes.explanation: Jerry's logical explanation for the confusion. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: "We did not want to antagonize the Nazis."
jokes.punchline: Treating Nazis as just another interest group not to offend
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry response treating Nazis like any other group to avoid conflict with. Brilliantly inappropriate politeness.

jokes.text: "I don't wanna make a Nazi unhappy."
jokes.explanation: George's conflict avoidance taken to absurd extreme. Perfect character moment in dark context.

jokes.text: "I am not O'Brien! I am not O'Brien!"
jokes.explanation: Climactic moment of George's panic. Good payoff though not particularly innovative.

jokes.text: Nazi casual 'Heil' bit - office small talk combined with Nazi salutes
jokes.punchline: Mundane office interactions with fascist salutes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant observational comedy about banality of evil. Mixing office small talk with Nazi imagery is darkly hilarious.

jokes.text: "Can I have one of those doughnuts?" with casual Heil
jokes.punchline: Mundane office request with fascist greeting
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the established bit, though not as strong as the setup.

tmdb_overview: Jerry and George take a limo meant for another, whose agenda includes more than just getting in from the airport.
